rows.Hints/Guidance:
💡 See Service Impact Analysis for further guidance 

Definition
📌 A SIA aims to determine and document the impact to business, or support units, utilizing a service should that service become impacted by a significant disruption to normal operations. This impact is utilized to define and justify the Criticality Tier (C1-C4), which includes the technology recovery objectives Recovery Time Objective (RTO) and Recovery Point Objective (RPO).

rows.BU Response:
❓ Is the lifecycle of your product or codebase tracked from development to retirement?
Answer: Yes, the lifecycle is tracked through multiple systems and processes.

SP STIs that are part of Product Pipeline Catalog are required to follow Security Operating Approval (SOA). SOA has process of decomission of STIs provided by ProdSec in their document: Supply Chain Exception Process"

Lifecycle Tracking Systems:
CMDB (ServiceNow)
✅ Purpose: Tracks STI metadata and business applications
✅ Lifecycle Stages: Active, in use, retired
✅ Features: Retirement date, Investment Objective, Status dependencies
✅ ESS Controls: Applied to STIs' business applications in CMDB
Catalog (Software Production)
✅ Purpose: Maintains STI metadata and monitoring information
✅ Tracking: Additional STI metadata beyond CMDB
✅ Maintenance: Maintained by Software Production team
Product Pipeline Catalog
✅ Purpose: Tracks STIs that are part of the productization pipeline
✅ SOA Process: Required for STIs in Product Pipeline Catalog
✅ Decommission: Process provided by ProdSec in Supply Chain Exception Process
GitLab CI Pipeline
✅ Purpose: Daily risk score recalculation for all STIs
✅ Tracking: Risk scores cached in Risk Assessment git repository
✅ Automation: Daily recalculation via GitLab CI pipeline
Lifecycle Coverage:
✅ Development - Tracked through SDLC and Git repositories
✅ Active Use - Tracked in CMDB and Catalog
✅ Maintenance - Tracked through ESS controls and risk scores
✅ Retirement - Tracked through SOA decommission process and CMDB retirement dates

❓ How is it tracked? (e.g., versioning, tagging, release tracking, roadmaps)?
Tracking uses version control, tagging, release tracking, and inventory systems.
SP uses a Change Management Database (CMDB) implemented in ServiceNow to store certain key STI metadata used in calculating risk scores (e.g. Criticality, userbase) and stores additional STI metadata in an internally developed software catalog (Catalog). Numeric risk scores for all STIs tracked by SP are recalculated daily by Catalog's GitLab CI pipeline (in our internal GitLab) and cached in a Risk Assessment git repository"
How Lifecycle is Tracked:
Versioning
✅ Git Repositories - Primary platform for code change management and version control
✅ GitLab Instance - Core repository hosting and policy enforcement
✅ Access and Audit History - Records maintained within Git repository and related services like Quay.io
Tagging
✅ GitLab - Tagging and version control through GitLab instance
✅ Quay.io - Container registry with tagging and versioning
✅ Release Tracking - Tagged releases tracked in Git repositories
Release Tracking
✅ SDLC Process - Systems Development Life Cycle tracks releases
✅ Code Review Process - Mandatory formal code review before merge/release
✅ GitLab CI Pipeline - Automated release and deployment tracking
Roadmaps
✅ Product Pipeline Catalog - Tracks STIs in productization pipeline
✅ CMDB - Investment Objective and Status dependencies
✅ SOA Process - Security Operating Approval tracks approval status and timelines
Inventory Systems
✅ CMDB (ServiceNow) - Stores key STI metadata (Criticality, userbase)
✅ Catalog - Additional STI metadata and monitoring information
✅ Risk Assessment Repository - Daily risk score recalculation and caching
Risk Tracking
✅ Daily Recalculation - GitLab CI pipeline recalculates risk scores daily
✅ Risk Assessment Repository - Cached risk scores in git repository
✅ Jira - Risk remediation or treatment work tracked in Jira

This table is updated programmatically multiple times per week. Actions / treatment plans are not in this table and are adressed in the next action item (#3).
https://gitlab.cee.redhat.com/catalog/risk-assessment/-/blob/main/services_risk_table.csv

Risk Treatment Plan
The consensus in SP is that we do not have a centralized report of only the security-focused work in Jira, or a filter that can easily distinguish these from other kinds of work being done in SP. Therefore our best representation of this would be the attestations and related communication on ESS controls in CMDB (in ServiceNow). I am working to provide PSC with visibility into the security attestations as right now they can only see high-level information and not the details within any particular attestation.

Risk Treatment Method
Risks identified through ESS review in ServiceNow require a mitigation plan, evidence that they have been mitigated, or an exception that must be approved by the reviewer. Risks identified by SP while developing and maintaining services are tracked in Jira using issues of the Risk type. These can include both security risks and business risks, for example implementation deadlines endangered by a significant scope change. Risks are highly visible in Jira and follow a status transition process until they are marked with a "done" state indicating that they have been addressed, mitigated, or accepted. Associates moving a risk to a "done" state explain through Jira comments how the risk was mitigated or addressed, or provide the justification for accepting the risk. Risks that require action from another team can be escalated through management.

Risk Tracking
SP uses a Change Management Database (CMDB) implemented in ServiceNow to store certain key STI metadata used in calculating risk socres (e.g. Criticality, userbase) and stores additional STI metadata in an internally developed software catalog (Catalog). Numeric risk scores for all STIs tracked by SP are recalculated daily by Catalog's GitLab CI pipeline (in our internal GitLab) and cached in a Risk Assessment git repository. Any risk remediation or treatment work is tracked in Jira.

Risks are highly visible in Jira and follow a status transition process until they are marked with a 'done' state indicating that they have been addressed, mitigated, or accepted. Associates moving a risk to a 'done' state explain through Jira comments how the risk was mitigated or addressed, or provide the justification for accepting the risk. Risks that require action from another team can be escalated through management."
Link: https://source.redhat.com/departments/operations/it-information-security/wiki/the_information_security_incident_response_plan_at_a_glance

Problem Management Process:
1. Recurring Issues:
✅ Tracked in Jira - Issues tracked using Jira issues of the Risk type
✅ Status Transition - Issues follow a status transition process
✅ Resolution Tracking - Issues marked as "done" when addressed, mitigated, or accepted
✅ Documentation - Associates explain how issues were mitigated or addressed through Jira comments
2. Root Cause Analysis (RCA):
✅ RCA Performed - Root cause analysis is done for incidents
✅ Future Improvement - RCA is used to improve future response
✅ Documentation - RCA examples can be provided (sanitized incident/RCA examples)
✅ Process Coverage - SP is covered by the RH Incident Response Process
3. Long-Term Fixes:
✅ Risk Tracking - Risks requiring design changes, fixes, or business action are tracked using Jira issues of the Risk type
✅ Treatment Plans - Risk remediation or treatment work is tracked in Jira
✅ Mitigation Plans - Risks identified through ESS review in ServiceNow require a mitigation plan
✅ Escalation - Risks requiring action from another team can be escalated through management
Tools Used:
Jira - Primary tool for tracking problems, risks, and long-term fixes
Risk type issues for risks requiring design changes, fixes, or business action
Status transition process until marked as "done"
ServiceNow - For ESS review risks and CMDB metadata
PagerDuty - For incident tracking (related to problem management)
Customer Portal/Salesforce - For customer/client support requests (if applicable)
